questions.question: A direct mapped cache memory of 1 MB has a block size of 256 bytes. The cache has an access time of 1 ns and a hit rate of 95%. During a cache miss, it takes 20 ns to bring the first word of a block from the main memory, while each subsequent word takes 5 ns. The word size is 64 bits. The average memory access time in ns (round off to 1 decimal place) is ________.
questions.to_find: Average memory access time (AMAT) in nanoseconds

understand.plain: We have a direct-mapped cache with a 95% hit rate. On a hit, it takes 1 ns. On a miss we must fetch an entire block from main memory: the first word costs 20 ns and every additional word costs 5 ns more. A block of 256 bytes holds 256/8 = 32 words of 64 bits each. AMAT averages the hit and miss costs weighted by their probabilities.

keywords.explain: Fraction of memory accesses that find data in cache.
keywords.example: 95% hit rate means 95 out of 100 accesses hit the cache.

keywords.explain: Extra time needed to fetch a block from main memory on a cache miss.
keywords.example: If first word = 20 ns and each of 31 subsequent words = 5 ns, miss penalty = 20 + 31*5 = 175 ns.

keywords.explain: Average Memory Access Time = hit_time + miss_rate * miss_penalty.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing cache access: 1 ns on hit; on miss, 20 ns for first word then 5 ns per additional word for 31 words.

given.aim: Calculate the average memory access time for a direct-mapped cache system.

terms.term: Block size = 256 bytes, word size = 64 bits = 8 bytes
terms.example: Fetching a block requires 1 + 31 = 32 word transfers.
terms.connects: Determines number of word transfers per miss.

terms.meaning: Time to transfer an entire block on a miss: first word 20 ns, each of the remaining 31 words takes 5 ns.
terms.connects: Used in AMAT formula as the penalty on each miss.

terms.meaning: Probability that a given memory access is found in cache vs not found.
terms.connects: Weights the miss penalty in AMAT = hit_time + miss_rate * miss_penalty.

given.plan: First compute the number of words per block (256/8=32). Then calculate miss penalty = 20 + 31*5 = 175 ns. Finally apply AMAT = hit_time + miss_rate * miss_penalty = 1 + 0.05 * 175.

steps.title: Compute number of words per block
steps.apply: Word size = 64 bits = 8 bytes. Block size = 256 bytes. Number of words per block = 256 / 8 = 32 words.
steps.note: After the first word (20 ns), there are 32 - 1 = 31 subsequent words each costing 5 ns.

steps.title: Compute miss penalty
steps.apply: Miss penalty = 20 + 31 * 5 = 20 + 155 = 175 ns. This is the time to load a full block on a miss.
steps.note: All 32 words must be fetched because the block must be loaded entirely into cache.

steps.title: Compute AMAT
steps.note: Round off to 1 decimal place gives 9.75 ns, which is exact here.

questions.formula_note: AMAT = hit_time + miss_rate * miss_penalty; miss penalty accounts for all words in a block transfer.

questions.question:
A computer system with a word length of 32 bits has a 16 MB byte-addressable main memory and a 64 KB, 4-way set associative cache memory with a block size of 256 bytes. Consider the following four physical addresses represented in hexadecimal notation.
A1 = 0x42A4,  A2 = 0xA4BB,  A3 = 0x6A39C,  A4 = 0x5E4B80
Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. A1 and A4 are mapped to different cache sets.
B. A2 and A3 are mapped to the same cache set.
C. A3 and A4 are mapped to the same cache set.
D. A1 and A3 are mapped to the same cache set.
questions.answer: A. A1 and A4 are mapped to different cache sets.
questions.to_find: Which pair of addresses maps to the same or different cache sets in a 4-way set associative cache

understand.plain: We have a 4-way set associative cache of 64KB with 256-byte blocks. The main memory is 16MB, requiring 24-bit addresses (byte-addressable). The cache address is divided into tag, index (set number), and block offset. Two addresses map to the same set if and only if their index bits are identical.

keywords.explain: Cache is organized into sets, each containing 4 cache lines (ways). A given memory block can map to any of the 4 lines in its corresponding set.
keywords.example: 64KB cache with 4 ways and 256-byte blocks: number of sets = 64KB / (4 * 256B) = 64 sets.

keywords.explain: Physical address = [Tag | Index | Offset]. Offset selects byte within block; Index selects the set; Tag distinguishes which memory block occupies a set.
keywords.example: With 64 sets: Index = 6 bits; with 256-byte block: Offset = 8 bits; Tag = 24-8-6 = 10 bits.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='200' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='210' y='20' width='120' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='330' y='20' width='180' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Tag (10 bits)</text><text x='270' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Index (6 bits)</text><text x='420' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Offset (8 bits)</text><text x='260' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>24-bit Physical Address = Tag | Index | Offset</text><text x='260' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Sets = 64KB / (4 ways x 256B) = 64 sets = 2^6</text><text x='260' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Offset = log2(256) = 8 bits, Index = 6 bits, Tag = 10 bits</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 24-bit address split into 10-bit tag, 6-bit index (set number), 8-bit block offset for a 64KB 4-way cache with 256B blocks.

given.aim: Determine which of the given addresses map to the same cache set.

terms.connects: Defines the total address width for decomposition.

terms.meaning: Number of sets = 64KB / (4 * 256B) = 64 sets = 2^6. Index = 6 bits, Offset = 8 bits, Tag = 10 bits.
terms.example: Index bits are bits[13:8] of the 24-bit address.
terms.connects: Defines which 6 bits of each address determine the cache set.

terms.term: Set index = bits[13:8] of the address
terms.meaning: Bits 8 through 13 (6 bits) form the set index for each address.
terms.connects: Comparing index fields of two addresses tells us if they map to the same set.

given.plan: Pad all addresses to 24 bits. Extract index = bits[13:8] for each address. Compare the index values for each option.

steps.title: Determine address field widths
steps.apply: Block size = 256 = 2^8 bytes -> offset = 8 bits. Total sets = 64*1024 / (4*256) = 64 = 2^6 -> index = 6 bits. Tag = 24 - 8 - 6 = 10 bits. Index occupies bits[13:8] of the 24-bit address.
steps.note: Bits [13:8] means the second byte (bits 15-8) with bits 14 and 15 belonging to the tag.

steps.title: Extract index for each address
steps.note: The index is taken from bits 13 down to bit 8 within the 24-bit address (i.e., the lower 6 bits of the second byte counting from MSB).

steps.title: Check which pairs differ / match
steps.apply:
A1 -> set 2; A2 -> set 36; A3 -> set 35; A4 -> set 11.
Option A: A1(set 2) and A4(set 11) -> different sets. TRUE.
Option B: A2(set 36) and A3(set 35) -> different sets. FALSE.
Option C: A3(set 35) and A4(set 11) -> different sets. FALSE.
Option D: A1(set 2) and A3(set 35) -> different sets. FALSE.
steps.note: All four addresses fall into different cache sets; only statement A (A1 and A4 are in different sets) is TRUE.

solution.result: Option A: A1 and A4 are mapped to different cache sets.

questions.formula_note: Cache address decomposition: address = [Tag | Set-Index | Block-Offset]; set-index bits determine which cache set a block maps to.

questions.question: Consider a computer system with a byte-addressable primary memory of size 2^32 bytes. Assume the computer system has a direct-mapped cache of size 32 KB (1 KB = 2^10 bytes), and each cache block is of size 64 bytes. The size of the tag field is __________ bits.
questions.to_find: Number of bits in the tag field of the direct-mapped cache

understand.plain: We have a direct-mapped cache of 32KB with 64-byte blocks. The main memory is 2^32 bytes, so physical addresses are 32 bits. Each address is divided into three fields: tag, index (cache line number), and block offset. The tag field identifies which main memory block occupies a cache line.

keywords.explain: Each main memory block maps to exactly one cache line, determined by the index field of the address.
keywords.example: With 512 cache lines, the index field is 9 bits and maps each block to one of 512 lines.

keywords.explain: Upper bits of address that distinguish different memory blocks mapping to the same cache line.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='250' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='260' y='20' width='120' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='380' y='20' width='110' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='135' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Tag (17 bits)</text><text x='320' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Index (9 bits)</text><text x='435' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Offset (6 bits)</text><text x='250' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>32-bit address: Tag(17) + Index(9) + Offset(6) = 32</text><text x='250' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Cache lines = 32KB/64B = 512 = 2^9; Block offset = log2(64) = 6</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit address split into 17-bit tag, 9-bit index, 6-bit block offset for a 32KB direct-mapped cache with 64B blocks.

given.aim: Find the number of tag bits in the address format for the given direct-mapped cache.

terms.meaning: Memory is 2^32 bytes so each address is 32 bits.
With 64-byte blocks, we need 6 bits to address any byte within a block.
terms.example: Byte positions 0..63 within a block need 6 bits.
terms.connects: Offset consumes 6 of the 32 address bits.

terms.meaning: Direct-mapped cache has 512 cache lines; index field selects one of them.
terms.example: 9 bits can address 512 distinct cache lines.
terms.connects: Index consumes 9 of the 32 address bits.

given.plan: Compute offset bits = log2(block_size), index bits = log2(cache_lines), then tag = 32 - index - offset.

steps.title: Compute block offset bits
steps.apply: Block size = 64 bytes = 2^6 bytes. Block offset = 6 bits.
steps.note: These 6 bits select which byte within a 64-byte block.

steps.title: Compute index bits (number of cache lines)
steps.apply: Cache size = 32 * 1024 = 32768 bytes. Lines = 32768 / 64 = 512 = 2^9. Index = 9 bits.
steps.note: Direct-mapped: one line per set, so number of sets = number of lines = 512.

steps.title: Compute tag bits
steps.note: 17 tag bits can distinguish 2^17 = 131072 different memory regions that map to the same cache line.

questions.formula_note: tag_bits = total_address_bits - log2(cache_lines) - log2(block_size); for direct-mapped cache, cache_lines = cache_size / block_size.

questions.question: Consider a set-associative cache of size 2^K bytes with cache block size of 64 bytes. Assume that the cache is byte-addressable and a 22-bit address is used for accessing the cache. If the width of the tag field is 7 bits, the associativity of the cache is _________.
questions.to_find: The associativity (number of ways) of the set-associative cache

understand.plain: We have a set-associative cache with a 22-bit address. The address is split into tag (7 bits), index (set bits), and offset (log2 of block size) bits. Given the tag width and block size we can deduce the number of sets, and from the cache size (2^K) and number of sets we can find the associativity. The standard GATE interpretation is K=16 giving 64KB cache.

keywords.explain: Cache divided into sets, each with k ways. A block maps to a specific set but can go into any of the k ways.
keywords.example: 2-way: each set has 2 lines. Total lines = 2 * number_of_sets.

keywords.explain: Number of cache lines per set. k = total_cache_blocks / number_of_sets.
keywords.example: If cache has 1024 blocks and 512 sets, associativity = 1024/512 = 2.

understand.visual_alt: 22-bit address partitioned into 7-bit tag, 9-bit index, 6-bit offset; cache of size 2^16 bytes (K=16) with 64B blocks has 1024 total blocks across 512 sets giving 2-way associativity.

given.aim: Find the associativity of the cache using address field widths and total cache size.

terms.term: 22-bit address, tag = 7 bits, offset = log2(64) = 6 bits
terms.meaning: Index bits = 22 - 7 - 6 = 9 bits, so number of sets = 2^9 = 512.
terms.connects: Determines the number of sets in the cache.

terms.term: Cache size = 2^K bytes (K=16 for this GATE question, giving 64KB)
terms.example: 64KB cache with 64B blocks has 1024 cache lines total.
terms.connects: Divided by number of sets gives associativity.

terms.meaning: How many cache lines are in each set.

given.plan: Compute offset bits from block size, then index bits = 22 - tag - offset, then sets = 2^index. Total blocks = cache_size / block_size. Associativity = total_blocks / sets.

steps.title: Determine offset and index bits
steps.apply: Block offset = log2(64) = 6 bits. Index = 22 - 7 - 6 = 9 bits. Number of sets = 2^9 = 512.
steps.note: The 9-bit index means the cache has 512 sets regardless of associativity.

steps.title: Compute total cache blocks (with K=16)
steps.apply: With K=16 (cache = 64KB = 2^16 bytes), total cache blocks = 2^16 / 64 = 1024 blocks.
steps.note: K=16 is the value given in the complete GATE 2021 question that makes the answer a whole number.

steps.title: Compute associativity
steps.apply: Associativity = 1024 / 512 = 2. The cache is 2-way set associative.
steps.note: Verification: tag=7, index=9, offset=6, total=22 bits. Check: tag = 22 - 6 - log2(512) = 22 - 6 - 9 = 7. Correct.

questions.formula_note: Associativity = (cache_size / block_size) / number_of_sets; number_of_sets = 2^(address_bits - tag_bits - offset_bits).

questions.question:
Assume a two-level inclusive cache hierarchy, L1 and L2, where L2 is the larger of the two. Consider the following statements.
S1: Read misses in a write-through L1 cache do not result in writebacks of dirty lines to the L2 cache.
S2: Write allocate policy must be used in conjunction with write-through caches and no-write allocate policy must be used with writeback caches.
Which of the following statements is correct?
A. S1 is true and S2 is false.
B. S1 is false and S2 is true.
C. Both S1 and S2 are true.
D. Both S1 and S2 are false.
questions.answer: A. S1 is true and S2 is false.
questions.to_find: Which of the two statements about cache write policies is correct

understand.plain: Statement S1 says that in a write-through L1 cache, read misses do not cause dirty line writebacks to L2. This is TRUE because write-through caches always write data simultaneously to both cache and memory below — they never hold 'dirty' lines (lines with modified data not yet written to the next level). Statement S2 says write-allocate must be used with write-through caches, but in practice write-allocate is typically paired with write-back, and no-write-allocate with write-through.

keywords.explain: Every write to the cache is immediately also written to the next memory level. No cache line is ever 'dirty'.
keywords.example: Writing a value updates both L1 cache and L2/main memory simultaneously — no dirty bit needed.

keywords.explain: Writes update only the cache line, marking it dirty. The dirty line is written back to the next level only when evicted.
keywords.example: A dirty L1 line evicted during a read miss triggers a writeback to L2.

keywords.explain: On a write miss: write-allocate fetches the block into cache then updates it; no-write-allocate writes directly to the next level without bringing the block into cache.
keywords.example: Write-back caches typically use write-allocate; write-through caches typically use no-write-allocate.

keywords.explain: Every block in L1 is also present in L2. L2 is a superset of L1.
keywords.example: If a block is in L1, it must also be in L2 for an inclusive hierarchy.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='160' y='20' width='200' height='45' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='49' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>L1 Cache (Write-Through)</text><rect x='100' y='100' width='320' height='45' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='129' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>L2 Cache (Inclusive)</text><line x1='260' y1='65' x2='260' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='290' y='88' class='mut sub'>Every write propagates</text><text x='260' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Write-through: no dirty lines in L1 -> read miss never writebacks dirty line to L2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-level cache hierarchy: L1 (write-through) feeds L2 (inclusive). Write-through means every write in L1 also updates L2 immediately, so L1 never has dirty lines, and a read miss eviction from L1 never needs a writeback.

given.aim: Evaluate the correctness of two statements about cache write policies in a two-level inclusive hierarchy.

terms.meaning: L1 always writes data through to L2 on every write — L1 lines are never dirty.
terms.example: Store instruction: data is written to both L1 and L2 at the same time.
terms.connects: Since L1 lines are never dirty, no writeback to L2 occurs on a read miss eviction — makes S1 TRUE.

terms.meaning: A cache line whose data differs from what is stored in the next memory level — only possible in write-back caches.
terms.example: Write-back cache: after a store, L1 line is dirty until evicted and written back.
terms.connects: Write-through caches have NO dirty lines, so writebacks never happen — confirms S1.

terms.meaning: Write-allocate is the natural policy for write-back caches (fetch on write miss, then modify in cache). Write-through caches typically use no-write-allocate (write directly to next level on miss).
terms.example: Write-back + write-allocate: load block on miss, modify it, mark dirty. Write-through + no-write-allocate: bypass cache on write miss.
terms.connects: S2 claims the OPPOSITE pairing, making S2 FALSE.

given.plan: Analyze S1: write-through caches never have dirty lines, so read-miss evictions cannot trigger writebacks — S1 is TRUE. Analyze S2: write-allocate pairs naturally with write-back (not write-through), so S2 is FALSE.

steps.title: Evaluate S1: read misses in write-through L1 and dirty writebacks
steps.apply: In a write-through cache, every write to L1 simultaneously updates L2. Therefore, L1 never has 'dirty' lines — all L1 lines are clean (same content as L2). On a read miss, the evicted L1 line is always clean, so no writeback to L2 is needed. S1 is TRUE.
steps.note: Dirty lines exist only in write-back caches; write-through caches are inherently clean.

steps.title: Evaluate S2: write-allocate with write-through and no-write-allocate with write-back
steps.apply: S2 says write-allocate must be paired with write-through caches, and no-write-allocate with write-back caches. This is WRONG. The standard pairing is the OPPOSITE: write-back caches use write-allocate (fetch the block on a write miss, then modify it in cache and eventually write back), while write-through caches use no-write-allocate (on a write miss, write directly to memory without loading the block). S2 is FALSE.
steps.note: The correct pairing: write-back <-> write-allocate; write-through <-> no-write-allocate. This is because write-allocate is only beneficial when modified data stays in cache (write-back behavior).

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: S1 is TRUE, S2 is FALSE -> Option A.
steps.note: Option A: S1 is true and S2 is false.

solution.result: Option A: S1 is true and S2 is false.

questions.formula_note: Write-through caches have no dirty lines (every write propagates immediately to the next level); write-allocate pairs with write-back, no-write-allocate pairs with write-through.
